sentences.text: Hanns Herman Saenger.
S-A-E-N-G-E-R.
Yes.
16Th of August 1919.
I was born in Berlin, in Germany.
Oh, my father must have got there [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] around about 1902 or '03.
And he met my mother there, yes.
I had a brother.
Gerald.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- so there were four of us.
My mother's name was Irma, and my father's name was Kurt.
Yes.
My mother's parents was-- were Julius and Hermine Eisenhardt.
And they came from Alsace-Lorraine.
And my father's parents were Herman Saenger-- he came from Inowrazlaw in Poland-- and my grandmother's name was Johanna Mushke.
And they got married in Chelmno in Poland.
No, well, only a nanny when we were young, and then a governess until we left.
Well, nanny brought us up as babies.
And the governess took over from her as we grew-- as I grew older.
Not that I was aware of.
Obviously, they were in the background.
But basically, it was done by the staff.
It was a very large apartment, about 10 or 12 rooms.
Very luxurious-- for the times.
And it was centrally situated in the residential area of Berlin-- it's called Charlottenburg.
Well, I mean, I only remember the time when I went to school.
Quite frankly, I don't remember going to the-- to primary school.
I remember going to the gymnasium.
No, it was government school.
It was the Kaiser Friedrich Realgymnasium.
It was a very well-known government school.
And school was in the mornings.
And the afternoons, there was no-- no such thing as sports at school.
In the afternoon and in winter, we went skating.
In summer, we played tennis.
We visited friends.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was a very peaceful existence.
Yes, orthodox.
Well, in a moderate way.
I mean, we kept Shabbat, we kept every Yom Tov.
And my father came from a very orthodox background.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother was-- her parents were less observant.
My grandmother kept kosher.
Oh, yes.
The rabbi's name was Rabbi Dr.
Selamonski.
And the shul was called the Luetzestrasse-- which is a very large, downtown shul.
Well, my father was a gabbai of the shul, as long as I can remember.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went to the occasional shiurim, probably once in ten days.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went to [NON-ENGLISH] shabbat.
Yes.
Well, it was a very structured, well-run community.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] If my memory serves me right, Germany, at that time, had something like just under 500,000 Jews.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the synagogues were very large.
They housed between 1,500 and 2,000 members each.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Jewish life was really organized, well-structured.
Well, there were educational programs.
There were shiurim there were-- for adults, for children, for teenagers.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jews didn't really mix with non-Jews, socially.
You felt more at home with your own people.
It was a very wealthy one.
I mean generally speaking, it was wealthy community.
Well, there were a number of Jewish neighborhoods.
I mean, I'm describing Berlin-- I don't really know what-- what Jewish life was like in the country, but it did exist in the country.
I never went.
I don't think-- I don't know whether they existed.
My father was a very wealthy man.
And he made his money from 1921 to '27, during the inflation in Germany when he [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] instinctively knew how to make the most of the conditions.
He exchanged the daily takings of his stores into gold coins daily, except on shabbat.
No, I don't think the stores were closed.
He didn't work.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And he came through that period of the inflation in Germany a very wealthy man.
He sold men's hats and caps, nothing else.
I don't really know.
But somewhere around 60 or 70 stores.
Yes.
I worked in a store every [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] free day that I had, I used to spend there.
My job was to put the hat-- the sold hats into a paper bag.
I remember that very well.
Yes.
No.
He wasn't interested.
No.
No.
My mother lived a very spoiled life.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She was not a well woman from 1928 onwards, and needed a lot of care.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She also came from a wealthy background, and she never had to work.
She died of cancer.
In 1934, in France.
German.
Well, it must be realized that in Germany, Jews were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] different in look to the Germans.
The Jews looked-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] well, the Jewish look was totally different to the Germanic look, which was the Aryan look, like the Swedes and the Norwegians and Danish people.
And in-- one of the things that made Hitler's rise easy, as far as anti-Semitism was concerned, the Jews stood out in a crowd.
People knew you were a Jew by looking at you because you looked different.
This is not the case in countries like France, or Spain, or any of the-- Italy.
But it was the case in Austria, and in Germany.
Absolutely.
I don't know.
I would guess--
Perhaps 10%, 12% of the-- of the class.
Mostly, yes.
Not directly.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you were made to feel different.
By both.
No, it was indirect.
But it was inferred.
Yes.
It's the same thing.

I mean, you-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] or perhaps my father coming from Poland, we were more sensitive to anti-Semitism than Jews that were of German extraction, because they hadn't lived through any anti-Semitic times.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And it's best illustrated by the fact that my father said, in 1928, that Germany's finished for Jews.
Hitler came out of prison, and published his book And he saw, very clearly, that anti-Semitism was going to be played up by Hitler.
And the Germans would swallow whatever he said.
And he started closing his stores, selling them-- usually to the managers of the respective stores.
Yes.
Absolutely.
By 1932, he had sold all but about six or eight of his stores.
At that stage, he kept it to himself.
But his general attitude was well known.
And people-- the average German Jew did not agree with him.
Yes.
No.
Not at all.
I was proud to be one.
No.
I saw myself taking over my father's business.
Yes.
Well, Hitler came in February, '33.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the year prior to him coming in, it was quite obvious that he was going to come in.
Hindenburg was going to appoint him chancellor.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was well-prepared, and the German nation reacts to military music, and marching, and clicking their heels, and flags hanging and so on.
Everyone wore an armband with a swastika on it.
And the posters started appearing widely-- don't buy from Jews, or this is a Jewish shop.
Oh, yes.
They smashed Jewish shop windows.
To give you an example, my father's business was called English Club before World War I.
And they smashed his stores, because they thought it was-- they were English-owned.
So he changed the name to Deutsche Hut-Vertrieb.
And when the Nazis came in, they smashed the shops, because they were called Deutsche, and he was a Jew.
So he changed it for the second time.
And he changed it to Der Hut-Vertrieb.
Which means 'the hat distributor.' And it meant he could retain the logo of the organization.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he couldn't do right.
I mean, it-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] anything that was called German could not be Jewish.
They wouldn't allow that.
The Nazis wouldn't allow it.
And they started burning books.
They started boycotting Jewish shops-- before Hitler became chancellor.
So that when he did become chancellor, things didn't really change.
The pace merely accelerated.
They suffered.
I mean, the Jews, the Jewish businesses suffered, definitely.

But the earlier part of Hitler's reign, the time that I was in Germany, it was nothing compared to probably from 1937, '38 onwards, when he really-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when he had published his final solution, which was to eliminate the Jews totally-- Jews, gypsies, homosexuals, and cripples.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Those were all to be eliminated.
And that's when he-- what I would-- the concentration camps, at first, were labor camps.
And they became concentration camps.
And finally, they became death camps.
And in fact, Chelmno was the first camp in Poland, where gas was used.
No, but we learned it.
Well, we were no longer in Germany then.
Well, I was saw little of it, because I was in a camp from April '33 until June '33.
And the day that I came out, we left Germany.
Well, his plans were to leave Germany in 1932.
By 1932, he had moved all his money officially, and through the official sources-- the banks-- into Switzerland.
And in 1932, the then finance minister Bruening brought out exchange control, which forbade any resident in Germany to have accounts outside Germany.
And my mother's health had deteriorated.
And she was operated in 1932.
And that's when they discovered that she had cancer.
And the doctors advised my father that he could not move her.
He would have to stay near the hospital where the professor who operated was.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] situation became so dangerous for my father that he said he must bring the money back.
He can't live in Germany, having transferred his money out officially through the banks.
It was known that he had done so.
So he brought his entire fortune back.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he made arrangements that should he ever leave, the French consul would move whatever cash he gave him for 25% into France.
Yes, definitely.
No, my father took all the cash that he had been able to accumulate from 1928 onward, and kept it in numerous private bank accounts.
And he kept transferring from one to the other.
And on every transfer he pulled some money out into cash, which he put into safes.
And so he had the larger portion of his money was in cash.
And he was able to move that.
But his fixed assets, of course, he lost-- he left behind.
Buildings, our apartment, the real estate that he owned, the factories that he owned.
Those he just lost.
Well, he refused to ever put a claim into-- he was not going to deal with the Germans.
Yes, he said in principle he was not prepared to deal with Germans.
When he died, I was an executor of his estate, together with a German Jewish lawyer.
And we decided that it was foolish not to put in claims.
Unfortunately, I never found his records of where his properties were situated.
But I knew some of the names.
And we were able through-- with the help of a German lawyer, after the war, to trace a number of properties.
And for those I got compensation.
It can never be adequate.
Only for what they were worth?
No, nothing like it.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] The-- the values that they paid out were minimal compared to the real value.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they're doing it now with East Germany.
I've got claims running in East Germany.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] What claims are you-- what you got in East Germany?
There's some real estate in East Germany that we were able to trace.

And we got a judgement in 1962 from the West German courts, to prove that the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] price that the Nazis paid for that building, or those buildings, was exactly the amount of money of the tax that Jews had to pay in order to leave Germany-- and had no relation at all to the value of the buildings.
So the court then gave me a ruling that this was an illegal sale, and I was to be refunded.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
When East Germany joined with Germany, the laws in East Germany were withdrawn, and the West German laws applied.
So now the East German claims are being processed.
Hopefully.
Yes.
It's on the lip of the gods.
No, I was 14.
When I was taken to camp, I was 13 and 1/2 years old.
Yes.
Very much so.
Through the media, through the Nazi papers, through the discussions at home-- discussions wherever you went.
I mean, you only heard Hitler or his-- his-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] senior officer speaking out in public meetings.
And you couldn't help but know what was going on.
